steps.content: Python's datetime module gives you the power to work with dates, times, and time intervals. It's part of the standard library — no extra installation needed. The two most important classes are datetime.date (year, month, day) and datetime.datetime (year, month, day, hour, minute, second).

import datetime

# Create a date
christmas = datetime.date(2024, 12, 25)
print(christmas)

# Create a datetime
party = datetime.datetime(2024, 12, 25, 20, 30)
print(party)
The datetime module is one of Python's most useful standard library modules. You'll reach for it whenever your program needs to know when something happens.
Getting the Current Date and Time
Use datetime.date.today() to get the current date, and datetime.datetime.now() to get the current date and time down to the microsecond. These are the two functions you'll use most often.

import datetime

today = datetime.date.today()
now = datetime.datetime.now()

print('Today:', today)
print('Now:', now)

The strftime method (string format time) converts a date or datetime into a formatted string. You provide a format string with special codes: %Y for 4-digit year, %m for 2-digit month, %d for 2-digit day, %A for full weekday name, and %B for full month name.

import datetime

d = datetime.date(2024, 12, 25)

print(d.strftime('%m/%d/%Y'))
print(d.strftime('%A, %B %d, %Y'))
print(d.strftime('%Y-%m-%d'))
Format codes are case-sensitive. %Y gives 2024 but %y gives just 24. %m gives 12 but %M gives minutes (00-59). Mixing them up is a common mistake!
Parsing Strings into Dates with strptime

The reverse of strftime is strptime (string parse time). It takes a string and a format, and returns a datetime object. This is how you turn user input or file data into real date objects you can work with.

import datetime

date_str = '2024-12-25'
parsed = datetime.datetime.strptime(date_str, '%Y-%m-%d')

print('Parsed date:', parsed.date())
print('Year:', parsed.year)

The timedelta class represents a duration — a difference between two dates or times. You can add or subtract timedeltas from dates and datetimes to calculate future or past dates. Create one with datetime.timedelta(days=7), then use + or -.

import datetime

today = datetime.date.today()
one_week = datetime.timedelta(days=7)
next_week = today + one_week
last_week = today - one_week

print('Today:', today)
print('Next week:', next_week)
print('Last week:', last_week)
You can also pass weeks, hours, minutes, and seconds to timedelta. For example, datetime.timedelta(hours=3, minutes=30) creates a duration of 3 and a half hours.
